course_description:
Modern developments in understanding natural processes of Earth’s surface and the impacts of human activities on environments applying geochemistry. Short, intensive modules covering theoretical background concepts, followed by hands-on practical exercises, including field activities. Additional fee may be payable on any field-based modules.

The course is designed as an introduction to environmental and exploration geochemistry. The focus is on geology,ﾠwith an overlay of how anthropogenic activities interact with the surficial environment. The surficial environment is affected by weathering processesﾠand human activities that, in turn, drive the dispersion of matter according to geochemical processes that characteristically occur at or near the Earth’s surface.ﾠﾠ

The course is very practical and includes field activities at Pinjarra Hills and Mt Coot-tha, where the students will put into practice the theory learnt in the lectures. Practical work includes exercises with stream and soil sample data sets, stream sediment and soil sample collection, and the evaluation of the analytical results obtained from these samples, using the software ioGas.ﾠThere isﾠa real geochemical survey at the mid-semester when students will be involved with collecting soil samples, submitting them to the lab, designing a Quality Assurance/Quality Control program (QA/QC), and learning how to interpret and present the geochemical results.

At the end of the course, students should have gained enough knowledge and experience to conduct a geochemical survey and interpret the results meaningfully way. We also aim to develop students' communication skills throughout the semester through group activities and presentations in class.

The skills learnt in this course would be extremely applicable for students aiming for a career inﾠthe environmental sector,ﾠﾠin the minerals industry or as a researcher in academia.

Additional timetable information
The details of the lectures and field trip schedules will be discussed with the students in class in week 1.
The fieldwork will be held at the Pinjarra Hills campus and Mt. Coot-tha. They are group work activities.
Attendance is not compulsory but highly recommended.
The activities conducted during the field work are linked to the assessments. Students who are unable to attend the fieldwork will be provided with information to complete the assessments.
Students will commute to Pinjarra Hills and Mt Coot-tha using public transport or private vehicles. There are no extra costs associated with the field trips.
course_aims: This course is aimed at advanced undergraduate and also graduate students who are interested in understanding the principles and practicalities of applying geochemistry in environmental investigationsﾠor exploration.ﾠ It focuses on the geochemical processes in the surficial environment; the dispersion or otherwise of elements going from rock to soil; and the role of water in controlling the chemical and physical behaviour of elements in the surficial environment. The course will be field focussed, and it will include the design and execution of soil and stream sediment surveys, the interpretation of geochemical results, and the statistical analysis of geochemical data. The students will have the opportunity to present their results and interpretations to the academic staff of the School of the Environment and to industry professionals.

learning_outcomes.description: Know how to use geochemical surveys in order to understand geochemical processes in the surficial environment.
Know how to collect geochemical information in the field.
Organise sample material, submit to a laboratory and ensure appropriate quality control in the process
Receive geochemical data, organise the data, interpret the data, report on the results with the aim of answering geological or environmental questions
Apply the knowledge gained to a real life situation
Make an oral presentation covering the collection, interpretation of data and results obtained in geochemical surveys.

assessment_details.title: Reports and presentations of practical activities (Pinjarra Hills soil sampling and Samarco case study)
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 1. Pinjarra Hills soil sampling report and presentation 8/09/2025 2:00 pm 2.Samarco case study: due date for the report 21/10/2025 2:00 pm
assessment_details.task_description: Task 1: Pinjarra Hills (Group Work) Students will dig a hole to collect soil samples, record the sample location using GPS, and describe the soil profile. Samples collected on different soil horizons will be analysed with a hand-held XRF. PhD and Eh will be measured on-site. The students will prepare a summary report (one report per group) that describes the sampling methodology, reports the results, and interprets the collected data. The report must be submitted via Turnitin. Task 2: Samarco Case Study (Group Work) The students will investigate the source of potentially toxic elements reported in the sediments of the Doce River after the failure of Samarco's tailings dam. They will review the literature and watch videos about the incident. There will be straightforward questions that must be answered and submitted in a Word document via Turnitin. The in-semester and the end of semester exams will contain questions about both tasks. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Online submission by Turnitin only by the due date and time. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Mt Coot-tha Geochemical Survey (Group Work)
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Poster
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students will investigate the stream sediments at Mt Coot-tha to determine if they contain potentially toxic elements above the environmental guidelines thresholds. Alternatively, they will have the option to explore the potential for gold mineralisation at Mt Coot-tha. They will design a stream sediment survey, execute the sampling, and submit the collected samples to a geochemical laboratory. They will then assess the geochemical data and prepare a report summarising the survey. Major outcomes will be presented in a poster session. The group report must be submitted via Turnitin. The poster must be submitted to the course coordinator via email. Students must print their poster and bring it to class on the designated presentation date. Only one student per group needs to submit the report on Turnitin. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Why Exploration and Environmental Geochemistry The introductory section addresses the cycles of matter and the definition of reservoirs and fluxes. It covers the concepts of primary and secondary geochemical halos, elemental associations in geological processes and pathfinder elements. The convergence of exploration and environmental geochemistry in the surficial environment is explained. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Review of minerals and rocks Lecture covering main rock and ore-forming minerals, and major igneous rock types, followed by a practical session on identifying the main types of rocks. This lecture is facultative for Earth Sciences students. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Weathering and erosion the drivers of dispersion - Drivers of metal mobility Factors that influence weathering (climate, bio-activity, rock type, geomorphology, time) and erosion (relief, rock type, climate and biota). Drivers of Matter’s Mobility in Aqueous Solutions Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Minerals after weathering Clays and Iron and Manganese Oxides/Hydroxide: heavy metals collectors in the surficial environment Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Chemical Weathering: From rock to soil The processes leading from rock to soil is dealt with by discussing the stability of rock-forming minerals and mobility of the major elements. What is soil? Different types of soil: soil classification Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Introduction to Microbiology Lectures and practical exercises introduce Microbiology and the' role of microprobes in weathering processes and elemental mobility. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Measurement of pH/Eh in soil - Pinjarra Hills Identification of soil profiles, soil description, sampling of soil horizons, measurement of Ph-Eh. Use of portable XRF to measure soil chemistry Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 8 (01 Sep - 21 Sep)
learning_activities.topic: Geochemical surveys: soil & stream sediments Lecture covering the practical aspects of soil and stream sediment sampling Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Introduction to IoGas Introduction to IoGas. Importa data, generate statistics and graphs. A database with soil samples is used in the practice. Learning outcomes: L04, L05
Designing geochemical surveys The context, design and value of geochemical surveys. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Mt Coot-tha stream sediment survey Collection of stream sediments and soil samples for geochemical analysis at the Mt Coot-tha Reserve. Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05
Quality assurance and quality control programs Lecture covering the concepts of QA/QC programs followed by a practical exercise Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05

Analytical Techniques Applicable analytical techniques are covered as well as ensuring quality control as viewed from exploration and environmental geochemists viewpoint when submitting samples to a commercial laboratory. Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 10 To Week 11 (06 Oct - 19 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Case Study-SAMARCO's taillings dam burst Assess SAMARCO's tailings dam burst and its impact on the environment from a geochemical point of view

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 12 (13 Oct - 26 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Evaluation of geochemical anomalies Application of basic statistics to identify errors in a geochemical database. Practical exercise in IoGas using univariate and multivariate statistics to identify geochemical anomalies. Lecture addressing the interpretation of geochemical anomalies from an environmental perspective. Introducing the legislation about assessment levels for contaminants (National Environment Protection (Assessment of Site Contamination) Measure 1999 Schedule B1. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05

Identification of geochemical anomalies Presentation about the selection of geochemical anomalies for further exploration by Rio Tinto's Principal Geochemist Michael Whitbread, followed by practical exercises using ioGas Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
Seminar about Acid Mine drainage Seminar about acid mine drainage formation presented by Dr Mansour Edraki (SMI) Learning outcomes: L01, L05

Group presentation: Findings of Mt Coot-tha survey Groups will present the findings of the geochemical survey in Mt Coo-tha to selected visitors and SEES's academics. Course wrap-up Learning outcomes: L05, L06
